text: The surgeon begins the operation by dissecting the gastrohepatic ligament. The left gastric artery is visible over the operative field, and the surgeon carefully skeletonizes it. Four clips are placed on the artery to ensure hemostatic control, followed by transection between the clips using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon identifies the right crus and carefully sweeps to separate the pillar of the right crus from the esophagus. The dissection continues over the anterior esophagus and extends to the pillar of the left crus.
The surgeon plans for an anterior partial wrap, avoiding posterior dissection of the esophagus to prevent exposure and potential injury to the posterior vagus nerve.

The surgeon exposes four to five centimeters of the esophagus below the diaphragm. A myotomy is performed over a six centimeter segment of the esophagus and extends to a two to three centimeter segment of the gastric wall.

The surgeon focuses on the mid body of the greater curvature of the stomach, where the greater omentum and short gastric arteries are transected to mobilize the fundus for the upcoming fundoplication.
The surgeon divides the fat pad at the gastroesophageal junction, revealing the complete dissection plane.

The surgeon initiates the esophagocardiomyotomy at the eleven o'clock position on the esophagus, approximately three to four centimeters above the gastroesophageal junction. The dissection is performed with precision to divide the longitudinal and circular muscle fibers while preserving the deeper mucosal layer.

The surgeon uses the blade of the harmonic scalpel for proximal dissection along the esophagus. For the thicker cardia of the stomach, a hook cautery is employed to dissect distally, pulling muscle fibers away from the mucosal layer to avoid thermal injuries.

The surgeon continues to divide the muscle layers of the esophagus, revealing the circular muscle fibers. Proximal fibers are transected using the harmonic scalpel, while near the gastroesophageal junction, the lower esophageal sphincter fibers are carefully dissected from the mucosal layer using fine-tipped scissors.

The surgeon completes the dissection, incorporating six centimeters of the esophagus and two to three centimeters of the gastric wall. Following this, an anterior partial fundoplication, known as door fundoplication, is performed.

The surgeon performs fundoplication by placing three sets of sutures. The first suture is applied to the left pillar of the cruci, incorporating the fundus of the stomach, the left cruce, and the muscle layers of the esophagus. The subsequent two sutures involve only the fundus of the stomach and the mucosal layers of the esophagus, providing coverage for the exposed mucosa.

The surgeon places the second set of sutures, incorporating the fundus of the stomach and the rim of the esophageal hiatus, to reduce tension on the fundoplication. The final set of sutures involves the right pillar of the cruce, with each suture incorporating the fundus of the stomach and the right cruz.
The surgeon completes the fundoplication, removes all needles and suture material, and disengages the liver retractor under direct visualization, marking the end of the operation.
